The encouraging note is recent: two Spain friendlies in 2026 returned a 7.3, his best rating in this sample. For a clean-sheet-grounded model that prizes consistency over flair, Simón profiles as a reliable, hard-to-beat presence behind a strong side rather than a match-winner in his own right.

J. Pickford.text: Pickford arrives at 31 as the settled custodian behind a side ranked fourth by FIFA, and his club record reads like a metronome: an ever-present 38 Premier League appearances in both 2024 and 2025, 3,420 then 3,432 minutes, with no rotation to speak of. The durability matters for a tournament where a first-choice keeper can play seven games inside a month. What gives slight pause is the trend in the numbers. His Everton league rating eased from 7.26 in 2024 to 7.02 in 2025, and his most recent international work has dipped further — 6.90 across two World Cup qualifiers and 6.75 over two friendlies in 2026. That is a soft decline rather than a collapse, and small samples in qualifying carry their own noise, but it nudges his level back toward the 6.8-7.0 band rather than the 7.2-plus peaks of his cup outings. For a deep England run, steady is the realistic expectation, not spectacular.

Y. Bounou.text:
Yassine Bounou heads to the tournament as a settled, in-form number one. His Al-Hilal league work has been steady rather than spectacular — 6.82 across 31 Pro League games in 2024, nudging up to 6.93 over 26 appearances in 2025 — but the pattern that matters here is how he raises his level on the big stage. He posted 7.42 in the AFC Champions League Elite in 2024 and a striking 7.78 across five FIFA Club World Cup outings in 2025, exactly the high-stakes, knockout environment a World Cup demands.

The Morocco numbers reinforce it: 7.17 over seven games at the Africa Cup of Nations and 7.33 in three 2026 friendlies. At 34 he is not declining, and a goalkeeper who consistently lifts his rating when the games get bigger is a quiet asset for a side ranked eighth in the world. For a team that may need to win matches by margins, that kind of reliability behind them is worth plenty.

M. Maignan.text:
Maignan arrives as the settled number one for the world's top-ranked side, and his club record reads like a metronome. Two near-identical Serie A campaigns frame him neatly: 37 appearances and 3,295 minutes at a 7.1 average in 2024, then 37 appearances and 3,303 minutes at a sharper 7.23 in 2025. That is a goalkeeper who plays nearly every week and is trending mildly upward, with his Champions League work (7.22 across ten games) and a standout 7.7 in the Coppa Italia underlining that the level holds against better opposition.

The mild caveat is the France shirt. His tournament football for the national side has been strong — 7.33 at the Euros, 7.34 in the Nations League — but the recent international samples are softer, a 6.62 in World Cup qualifying and 6.83 across three 2026 friendlies. For a France team modelled as a genuine contender, his club consistency is the reassuring signal; the dip in recent national-team ratings is the small open question worth watching.

Alisson Becker.text:
Alisson's club numbers tell a story of gentle decline from a very high base. His 2024 was his strongest run, a 7.11 average across 28 Premier League games and a standout 7.62 over six Champions League nights. The 2025 league campaign softened that picture: 26 appearances at 6.78, his weakest sustained rating in the dossier, even as his European form held firmer at 7.18. The encouraging signal for Brazil is that he has rediscovered something on international duty, posting 7.18 across nine World Cup qualifiers — comfortably above his recent Liverpool league baseline and right back at his 2024 Copa America level.

For a 33-year-old goalkeeper, that mix of a slightly dipping club floor and a solid qualifying ceiling reads as steady rather than commanding. Brazil sit sixth in the FIFA ranking, and Alisson remains a dependable rather than spectacular last line — enough to keep his side competitive, short of the form that single-handedly wins knockout ties.

Diogo Costa.text:
Costa is Porto's undisputed number one and the trend is gently upward. Across 2024 he logged 32 Primeira Liga starts at a 6.98 rating, with a slightly softer 6.89 in the Europa League. The 2025 season tightened that picture: 33 league appearances and 2,907 minutes at 7.07, plus 7.05 in Europe and a tidy 7.33 across his Taça de Portugal outings. There's no drama in these numbers, which for a goalkeeper is exactly the point — he plays nearly every minute and his form holds steady rather than spiking or sagging.

The international sample flatters him further. He posted 7.34 at the Euros and 7.23 in the Nations League, both comfortably above his club baseline, even if a 6.60 across six World Cup qualifiers is a reminder that the floor exists. For a team ranked fifth in the world, that combination — durable, rarely below 7, and demonstrably capable of raising his level on the biggest stage — is close to ideal heading into the tournament.

T. Courtois.text:
Courtois remains Real Madrid's undisputed number one, and his form holds up across the highest level of club football. In La Liga his rating slipped slightly, from 7.11 across 32 games in 2024 to 6.93 over a similar 2,880-minute load in 2025, but the bigger games tell a better story: he posted 7.43 in the 2024 Champions League and backed it up with 7.29 in 2025, plus a tournament-best 7.47 across six matches at the FIFA Club World Cup. For a 33-year-old keeper, that consistency under elite pressure is the headline, and it travels well into knockout football.

For Belgium, ranked ninth by FIFA, that matters. His two 2026 friendlies returned a steady 7.05, and his qualifying work sat at 6.93 over four games. The pattern is clear and encouraging: a goalkeeper who raises his level when the stakes climb. Against the better attacks Belgium will face, Courtois is the kind of margin-shifting presence the model rates highly.

T. Hayakawa.text:
Tomoki Hayakawa has been a model of consistency in goal for Kashima, and the trend is the encouraging part. He played every minute he could in the J1 League across 2024 and 2025 — 38 appearances and 3,420 minutes in each — and his rating climbed from 7.06 to 7.15. The 2026 season has him rising again: 7.38 across 17 league outings, comfortably his best return and the kind of form that earns a goalkeeper a hard look ahead of a tournament.

The caveat is at international level, where the picture is thinner. His Japan minutes have come mostly in the EAFF E-1 Championship and friendlies, and his rating there (6.64 in the larger sample) sits below his club number, including an eight-minute friendly cameo in 2026. At 26 and 187cm, the profile is solid and improving, but his ceiling for the World Cup likely depends on whether that club form translates against stiffer opposition than he has faced for the national side so far.

O. Baumann.text:
Baumann arrives at 35 with a heavy recent workload but a quietly slipping line on the chart. His 2024 was his steadiest in a Hoffenheim shirt, a 7.01 Bundesliga rating across 28 games, backed by solid international cameos — 7.00 in the Nations League and 6.92 over six World Cup qualifiers. The 2025 league season was busier still, a full 34 appearances and 3,060 minutes, but the rating eased to 6.81, the marks of a goalkeeper playing a lot rather than playing better.

The flag for the tournament is the 2026 sample: three friendlies, 270 minutes, a 6.27 average that sits well below anything in his club record. It is a small sample and friendlies are friendlies, but for a 35-year-old keeper behind a Germany side ranked tenth, the trend points down rather than up. The dossier shows a dependable, high-volume career number drifting the wrong way at exactly the wrong moment, which is reason for measured caution rather than confidence.

preview.text:
Home advantage doesn't get much more emphatic than the Azteca, and the numbers frame this as a clear mismatch. Mexico arrive 15th in the world and worth €191.85m to South Africa's €49.25m, riding a run capped by a 5-1 friendly win over Serbia, while Bafana have stumbled through draws with Nicaragua and Jamaica and a March loss to Panama. The model reads it accordingly: 2.06 expected goals for the hosts against just 0.56, a 72.2% Mexico win and only 8.6% for South Africa. Mexico are favoured to open the scoring 72.5% of the time, and the cleanest outcomes on the board are 2-0 (15.4%) and 1-0 (14.5%), with both teams scoring rated a modest 38.1%.

Where the model and the market part company is on the margins of certainty. Polymarket has Mexico priced at a flat 100 cents — effectively no chance for the draw or an upset — whereas the model still leaves almost 28% with South Africa avoiding defeat. On goals the gap flips: the market sits firmly under 2.5, while the model has over 2.5 at a near coin-toss 48.8%. So treat the winner as well-signposted but not sealed, and the total as a genuinely open question rather than the formality the market implies.

review.text:
Mexico opened their World Cup at the Estadio Azteca with a controlled 2-0 win over South Africa, the kind of result the numbers had been pointing at all along. It was a clean continuation of the home side's recent form, and the scoreline landed exactly on the model's single most likely outcome: 2-0 sat top of the projected scores at 15.4 percent, just ahead of 1-0. A 2.06 expected-goals figure for Mexico against 0.56 for South Africa described this game well in advance, and the shutout chimed with a below-even both-teams-to-score read of 38 percent.

This was a good night for both the model and the market, with little to separate them. The model priced Mexico at 72.2 percent to win; Polymarket had them at 69.5 cents — close agreement that South Africa, ranked 60th to Mexico's 15th, would struggle to find a foothold. The locked bracket pick of 1-0 Mexico nailed the result if not the exact margin, and a first-to-score read of 72.5 percent for the hosts captured how the game was always likely to flow. A straightforward call, and a correct one.

preview.text:
South Korea host the Czech Republic in Guadalajara as modest favourites in a matchup the model reads as close. The projected goals — 1.46 to 0.98 — tilt Korean, and that feeds a 47.4% home win against 24.9% for the visitors, with the draw a live 27.7%. The reasoning is partly venue and partly that Korea, ranked 25th to the Czechs' 41st, carry the edge despite trailing on squad value (€139m to €188m). Both sides arrive in form, Korea off back-to-back friendly wins, the Czechs unbeaten across their last nine, which helps explain why the model sees a tight, low-event game: over 2.5 goals lands at just 44%, both teams to score at 48.6%, and the likeliest scores are 1-1 (13.1%) and 1-0 (12.1%).

The gap with the market is stark rather than marginal. Polymarket prices this as a certainty across the board — home win at 100 cents, over 2.5 goals at 100, both teams to score at 100 — where the model treats every one of those as closer to a coin flip or worse. That is not confirmation of either view; it is a genuine disagreement. The model's numbers say this is a contest the Czechs can win or draw, and the market's all-or-nothing pricing leaves no room for that.

review.text:
South Korea opened their Group A campaign with a 2-1 win over the Czech Republic in Guadalajara, turning a tight contest into the kind of narrow home victory the numbers leaned toward without ever promising. The Czechs, the more valuable squad on paper at €188m to Korea's €139m and unbeaten in their previous nine before kickoff, got on the scoresheet but couldn't take a point from the Estadio Akron.

Grade this one a clean hit for the model. It made South Korea favorites at 47.4% to win, with the draw at 27.7% and the Czechs trailing on 24.9%, and the result landed squarely on the right side. The exact 2-1 scoreline was the fifth-likeliest line at 9.1%, and with an xG read of 1.46 to 0.98 the model expected Korea to edge the goal count without a blowout — which is exactly what happened. BTTS came in too, just ahead of the model's near-coin-flip 48.6%. Our locked bracket pick, a 1-1 that called the home result correctly, also graded as a hit. A solid, unflashy night for the projections.

preview.text:
Canada open their home World Cup at BMO Field as heavy favourites, and the model leans into it: 2.36 expected goals to Bosnia's 0.57, a 76.97% home win, and a forecast that Canada strike first 75.8% of the time. The most likely scorelines are all clean sheets — 2-0 at 14.8%, 1-0 at 12.2%, 3-0 at 11.6% — which tells you how the numbers read this: a hosting side with the ranking edge (30th to 65th) and the squad value (€198.65m to €151.6m) controlling territory against opponents the model expects to struggle to score. BTTS sits at just 39.9%.

The disagreement with the market is the real story. Polymarket prices Canada at only 53.5 cents to win, with the draw at 27.5 and Bosnia as live as 20.5 — far above the model's 6.92% for an away win. The market clearly respects Bosnia's qualifying pedigree, including those results against Italy and Wales, in a way the model does not. The two also part on goals: the model has over 2.5 at 56.1% against the market's 42.5. So treat this as genuinely open at the margins — the model sees a comfortable, possibly emphatic Canadian win; the market sees a contest Bosnia can disrupt.

review.text:
Canada and Bosnia and Herzegovina shared a 1-1 draw at BMO Field, a result that quietly punctures the model's confidence. The numbers had painted Canada as overwhelming favourites at home: a 76.97% win probability, an expected goals edge of 2.36 to 0.57, and a three-in-four chance of scoring first. Instead Bosnia, who arrived unbeaten across their last six qualifiers, found the goal that turned a projected rout into a point apiece. The draw was priced at just 16.11%, and both teams scoring was rated only 39.93%, so the way this game played out sat firmly in the model's blind spot.

That makes for an honest miss to grade. The locked bracket pick was 2-0 Canada, the single most likely scoreline at 14.81%, and it landed nowhere near — the actual 1-1 ranked only fifth on the model's board at 7.58%. The market echoed the same heavy lean toward the hosts, so neither view comes out of this looking good. Bosnia's recent form, including draws with Italy and Austria, perhaps deserved more respect than a 6.92% away price allowed. A clear reminder that 77% is not 100%.

preview.text:
The United States open Group D at home in Los Angeles as clear favourites, and the model leans into it: an expected 1.58 goals to Paraguay's 0.82, a 54.6% win probability against 26.4% for the draw and 18.9% for Paraguay. The shape of the matchup explains the gap — a squad valued at €385.65m against Paraguay's €153.65m, a side ranked 16th hosting one ranked 40th, and the memory of a 2-1 US win when these two met as recently as last November. The most likely scoreline is a tight 1-0 at 13.7%, with 1-1 close behind at 12.4%, so this reads as a controlled home win rather than a rout: the US are 59.4% to score first but only 43% of games here clear 2.5 goals.

Where it gets interesting is the moneyline. The market is more cautious about the hosts, pricing them at 47.5 cents against the model's 54.6%, and crediting Paraguay at 24.5 cents versus the model's 18.9%. That is a genuine disagreement worth respecting — the US friendly form is patchy, with losses to Germany, Portugal and Belgium among recent home games. On totals and both teams to score the two sides essentially agree, so the open question is purely how much resistance Paraguay offer.

review.text:
The United States ran out 4-1 winners over Paraguay at SoFi Stadium, a scoreline that comfortably beat the run of pre-match expectation. The model had the hosts as clear favourites at 54.61% with an xG edge of 1.58 to 0.82, and they delivered on the direction emphatically. Both teams scoring was no surprise either, landing right around the 45% the model gave it, and this repeats the pattern from November, when the USA edged Paraguay 2-1 in a friendly.

So the headline call was right: home win backed at 54.61% by the model and 49.5 cents by Polymarket, and a result that arrived. The miss was magnitude. Five goals blew past a market that priced over 3.5 at just 22.12% and over 4.5 at 9.58%; the most likely single scoreline was 1-0 at 13.72%, with the locked bracket pick a cautious 1-1. That graded as a hit on result, but the model never saw a four-goal home haul coming. Credit the lean, dock the totals — the USA were simply more ruthless than the numbers suggested.

preview.text:
This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it as one. Switzerland, ranked 19th and carrying a squad valued at €332.5m against Qatar's €19.93m, are made 80.9% favourites, with the draw at 13.1% and Qatar held to just 6.1%. The expected-goals split tells the same story: 2.78 for the Swiss, 0.67 for Qatar, a gap that flows naturally from recent form. Qatar arrive without a goal in their last two friendlies and have lost four of their last ten, while Switzerland have won five of theirs and put four past both Jordan and Kosovo. The model has the Swiss favoured to score first 77.8% of the time and to lead at the break 60.6%, with 0-2 (12.3%) and 0-3 (11.4%) its likeliest scorelines.

On the result the market barely budges from the model — Polymarket's 80.5% on Switzerland and 13.5% draw are near-identical. The honest disagreement is on goals. The model sees over 2.5 landing 66.9% of the time and both teams scoring at 45.9%; the market is more cautious at 57.5% and 37.5%. That leaves a genuine open question about how freely this game flows, even if nobody doubts who wins it.

review.text:
Qatar held Switzerland to a 1-1 draw at Levi's Stadium, a result that lands among the least likely outcomes on the board. Both sides scored, which the model gave a 45.9% chance, but the bigger story is that Qatar avoided defeat at all. Switzerland, ranked 19th and carrying a squad worth €332.5m against Qatar's €19.93m, were supposed to walk this. The 2.78 expected goals our model handed them never materialised, and the side that lost 0-3 to Tunisia and drew 0-0 with El Salvador last week found a point against far stronger opposition.

This was a clear miss for both the model and the market, and there's no dressing it up. We had Switzerland at 80.86% to win with the draw at just 13.07%; Polymarket sat almost identically at 79.5% and 13.5%. Our locked bracket pick of 0-2 to Switzerland graded out wrong on both score and result. The favourites being throttled to a single goal is exactly the kind of variance an 80% line is supposed to absorb occasionally — today it bit. Credit Qatar; the numbers all pointed the other way.

preview.text:
This is a heavyweight group-stage opener that the model reads as far tighter than the matchup might suggest. Brazil sit sixth in the world to Morocco's eighth, and carry nearly double the squad value at €928.2m against €498.3m, but the expected-goals split is modest: just 1.06 to 0.82 in Brazil's favour. That feeds a cagey set of probabilities — Brazil home at 39.9%, the draw a hefty 33.1% and Morocco still live at 27.0% — with 0-0 actually the single most likely scoreline at 16.0%. Morocco arrive on a serious run, with an AFCON campaign full of clean sheets behind them, and the model clearly respects their organisation: both teams to score is priced at only 37.2%.

Where the model and the market part ways is on Brazil's standing. Polymarket has the favourites at 59.5 cents, almost twenty points clear of the model's read, and trims Morocco to 16.5 against the model's 27.0. The totals tell the same story: the market sees over 2.5 goals at 46.5%, the model far lower at 29.0%. So this is a genuine open question — the market backs Brazilian class to tell, the model expects a low-scoring, closely fought night, and one of them will be graded wrong.

review.text:
Brazil and Morocco shared the points at MetLife Stadium, a 1-1 draw that left the model looking shrewder than the market. Both sides found the net, an outcome the model had pegged at just 37 percent for both-teams-to-score, yet the actual scoreline of 1-1 sat third on its list of likeliest results at 13.9 percent. The model's expected goals of 1.06 to 0.82 pointed to a tight, low-scoring affair, and that is exactly what arrived.

On the result itself, the gap between model and market matters. The model treated this as a genuine open question, giving Brazil only 39.9 percent and the draw a hefty 33.1 percent, while Polymarket priced Brazil as clear favourites at 58.5 cents and the draw at just 24.5. The split point landed on the model's side: Morocco, eighth in the world and unbeaten across their last ten, held the favourites. Our locked bracket pick, a 0-0 Brazil edge, missed on both score and result, predicting a home win where a draw materialised. Credit the model's caution; the market overrated Brazil here.

preview.text:
Scotland arrive as favourites and they look it on paper: ranked 43rd to Haiti's 83rd, with a squad valued at €170.25m against €55.9m, and both sides land in Boston in good touch after 4-0 wins in early June. The model leans Scotland's way without committing to it, projecting 1.58 expected goals to Haiti's 1.19 and pricing the away win at 45.87%, the draw at 26.08% and a Haiti win at 28.05%. That is a clear edge but a long way from a formality, and the scoreline spread reflects it — 1-1 tops the chart at 12.38%, with 0-1 and 1-2 close behind, the picture of a tight game Scotland are expected to nudge rather than dominate.

This is where the market parts company with us. Polymarket has Scotland at 62 cents, a full sixteen points clearer than the model's 45.87%, and trims Haiti to just 16 cents against our 28.05%. The two broadly agree on goals — over 2.5 sits at 52.23% to the market's 57.5%, and both-teams-to-score near the coin-flip at 55.74% versus 53.5% — so the disagreement is purely about how safe Scotland's win is. The model says live underdog; the market says comfortable. Treat that gap as the genuine open question here.

review.text:
Scotland edged Haiti 1-0 at Gillette Stadium, a tight, low-scoring afternoon that gave the favourites their three points without ever turning into the comfortable win their squad value and ranking might suggest. A single goal settled it, the kind of margin that fit a fixture both the model and the market read as Scotland's to lose but not by much.

On the call, this was a solid night. The model made Scotland the pick at 45.9% to win, against 28.1% for Haiti and 26.1% for the draw, and the away result duly landed — and the locked bracket pick had the right winner even if the scoreline was off. Polymarket leaned harder still, pricing Scotland at 62 cents, and that confidence was rewarded. Where the numbers missed was on goals: the model gave the over 2.5 a coin-flip 52.2% and both teams to score 55.7%, yet a clean sheet and a single strike beat both. The exact 0-1 was the second-likeliest scoreline at 9.4%, so even the surprise wasn't really one. A good day for the lean, a quieter one for the goal count.

preview.text:
Played at neutral BC Place but nominally Australia's match, this is a tight one on paper: the model gives the hosts a 1.24 to 1.15 expected-goals edge and prices the three results almost evenly, with Australia at 37.7%, the draw at 29% and Turkey at 33.4%. That reflects a low-scoring, balanced contest — 1-1 is the single likeliest scoreline at 13.7%, over 2.5 goals is closer to a coin-flip at 42.9%, and both teams to score sits at 49.3%. The shape is two sides that grind: Australia have ground out narrow wins over New Zealand, Canada and Cameroon, while Turkey arrive on a strong run, beating Venezuela and thrashing FYR Macedonia 4-0.

The real story is the gap between the model and the market. Polymarket sees this very differently, making Turkey clear favourites at 57.5 cents and pushing Australia all the way down to 17.5 — far below the model's 37.7%. That's a chasm, and the market has reasons the model doesn't fully weigh: Turkey rank 22nd to Australia's 27th, and their squad is valued at €473.7m against just €77.45m. On totals the two broadly agree, but on who wins this is a genuine open question, and the disagreement is stark.

review.text:
Australia took this one in front of a Vancouver crowd, winning 2-0 to claim the points against a Turkey side that arrived with all the pre-match billing. The two goals split the model's totals neatly: over 1.5 was favoured at 69.7% and landed, while over 2.5 (42.9%) did not, a low-event game roughly in line with the model's modest 1.24-1.15 expected-goals read.

This was a clean win for the model over the market. Our numbers framed the match as a near coin-flip with Australia nudged ahead at 37.7% to Turkey's 33.4%, and the locked bracket pick correctly called a home result. Polymarket saw it very differently, pricing Turkey as firm favourites at 56.5 cents and writing Australia off at just 17.5 — a reading that looked reasonable on squad value, with Turkey's €473.7m dwarfing Australia's €77.5m, but proved badly wrong on the night. Credit where due: the model trusted Australia's recent home form and resisted the market's lean. The honest caveat is that a 37.7% favourite winning is no triumph of certainty, only a sensible call paying off.

preview.text:
This is about as lopsided as a World Cup group game gets. Germany, ranked 10th and carrying a squad worth €947m against Curaçao's €25.78m, arrive in Houston on a near-perfect run — nine wins in their last ten, including a 6-0 over Slovakia in qualifying and a 2-1 friendly defeat of the USA last week. Curaçao, 82nd in the world, have looked solid in CONCACAF but porous against bigger sides, shipping four to Scotland and five to Australia in recent friendlies. The model reads it accordingly: 3.5 expected goals for Germany against just 0.55, an 89.95% home win, and a clear favourite to score first at 84.8%. The likeliest scorelines are all routs — 3-0, 4-0 and 2-0 cluster at the top.

Where it gets interesting is the margin of disagreement. Polymarket is even more bullish on Germany, pricing the win at 94 cents to the model's 90, and leans harder into goals too — 82.5% on over 2.5 versus the model's 76.9%, and 45.5 cents against 38.1 on over 4.5. The one place the model pushes back is both teams to score, at 41.2% against the market's 32.5%, a hint it credits Curaçao with more of a puncher's chance than the traders do.

This is the dossier's most interesting disagreement, and it sits right in the result market. On paper Netherlands carry the pedigree — seventh in the world, a squad valued at €754m against Japan's €271m — but the model sees a coin flip leaning slightly the wrong way for the favourites. It splits the expected goals almost evenly at 1.21 to 1.26 and actually makes Japan marginal favourites, 36.9% to win against 34.6% for the Dutch, with the draw at 28.5%. Japan's record backs that read: wins over England, Scotland and Brazil dot a long unbeaten stretch, while Netherlands lost at home to Algeria as recently as 8 June. It shapes up as a tight, low-event game — 0-0 is the fourth-likeliest score at 9.1%, and 1-1 leads the board at 13.5%.

That is where Polymarket parts company with the numbers. The market prices Netherlands at 47.5 cents, a clear favourite, and pushes Japan all the way down to 26.5 — almost the mirror image of the model's lean. Both sides agree this should be a cagey watch: over 2.5 goals sits at 44.9% on the model and 47.5 in the market, with both-teams-to-score around the coin-flip mark. The totals are settled; the winner is a genuine open question, and the model thinks the market is underrating Japan.

This is a low-scoring chess match by both readings, but the model leans harder into that than the market does. Ecuador's defensive solidity — clean sheets against Canada, Paraguay and a 1-0 win over Argentina dot their recent form — pairs with an Ivory Coast attack the model rates at just 0.69 expected goals to 0.83 for the visitors. That edge tips Ecuador to a 35.5% win against the Ivorians' 27%, but the model's real conviction is the draw at 37.6%, and the single most likely result is a goalless 0-0 at 22.5%. Ecuador are favoured to score first (42.5%) and the contest is expected to be cagey from the start, with a level half-time the runaway favourite at 57.3%.

Where model and market part ways is the goals total. The model sees under 2.5 as comfortably the bet, pricing over 2.5 at just 19.6% and both teams to score at 28.7%. Polymarket is far more open on both, marking over 2.5 at 32.5 cents and BTTS at 41.5 cents — a meaningful gap that says the market expects a livelier game than the numbers do. The moneyline is closer to agreement, with Polymarket at 37.5 away, 32.5 draw and 29.5 home. Treat the goals question as genuinely open.

Sweden go in as modest favourites at the Estadio BBVA, and the gap is real but narrow. The model gives them 1.22 expected goals to Tunisia's 0.96, which translates to a 41.4 percent win, with the draw at 30.2 and Tunisia at 28.4. That is the profile of a tight, low-scoring group game rather than a mismatch, and the scoreline distribution backs it up: 1-1 leads the board at 13.9 percent, just ahead of 1-0 at 13.1 and a 0-0 at 11.9. On paper Sweden hold the edge in squad value, 406m euros to roughly 70m, but recent form offers little comfort to either side, so a cagey night with under 2.5 goals landing 62.6 percent of the time feels the honest read.

Where it gets interesting is the disagreement with the market. Polymarket prices Sweden at 50.5 cents, a clear nine points above the model's 41.4, and trims both the draw and Tunisia accordingly. The market also leans for goals, pricing over 2.5 at 44.5 against the model's 37.4 and both teams to score at 48.5 versus 44.4. So the bettors see a more comfortable, more open Sweden win; the model sees a coin-flip-adjacent grind. Treat it as a genuinely open question.

This is a heavy mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. Spain, ranked second in the world with a squad valued at €1.22bn against Cape Verde's €54.5m, come in on a strong run — five straight qualifying wins including 6-0 at Türkiye and 4-0 at Georgia, with only friendly draws denting the record since. The Dixon-Coles engine projects 2.98 expected goals for Spain and just 0.41 for Cape Verde, which translates to an 88.1% Spain win and a tidy clean-sheet feel: Spain are 84.9% to score first and the most likely scorelines are 2-0 (15.0%), 3-0 (14.9%) and 4-0 (11.1%). Both teams to score sits at just 31.8%, reflecting how little the model fancies Cape Verde's threat despite their own recent run of clean sheets against Bermuda and Serbia.

The market broadly agrees but is even firmer on Spain, pricing the home win at 90.5 cents against the model's 88.1, and shading the draw down to 6.75 cents from the model's 9.3. On goals the two are close — over 2.5 is 65.7% on the model and 69.5 on Polymarket — so the only real daylight is in how completely the market dismisses any Cape Verde route to a point. That gap is small, but it's where the model is fractionally more cautious.

Belgium arrive in Seattle as comfortable favourites, and the shape of this one is straightforward: a UEFA side ranked ninth, with a squad valued near €547.5m, against an Egypt team ranked 29th and worth roughly a fifth of that. The model gives Belgium 1.5 expected goals to Egypt's 0.71 and makes them 55.65% to win, with the draw at 27.36% and Egypt at just 16.99%. The forecast leans on Belgium's scoring weight — recent friendly wins of 5-0 over Tunisia and 2-0 in Croatia fit a team that piles up goals — while Egypt's defensive record, including a 0-0 draw in Spain, hints at a side that can frustrate. The most likely scorelines are tight: 1-0 at 15.96%, 2-0 at 12.38%, with 0-0 still live at 11.63%.

That last point is where model and market part ways. Polymarket prices Belgium a touch shorter at 60.5 cents, but the bigger gap is on goals: the market sees over 2.5 at 48.5% against the model's 37.8%, and both teams to score at 48.5% versus the model's 39.88%. So traders expect a more open, higher-scoring game than the numbers do. With Egypt's low expected output, that disagreement is the genuine open question here.

Uruguay arrive in Miami as heavy favourites, and the numbers explain why. Ranked 17th to Saudi Arabia's 61st, with a squad valued at €359.3m against €40.68m, they are the side the model expects to control this game. The expected goals split tells the story bluntly: 1.57 for Uruguay, just 0.43 for Saudi Arabia. That feeds a 65.6% Uruguayan win probability, a 25.2% draw and only a 9.3% chance the Saudis take all three points. Uruguay are favoured to open the scoring 67.7% of the time, and the most likely scorelines are 0-1 (20.8%) and 0-2 (16.7%), with a goalless draw third at 14%. This reads as a tight, low-event match Uruguay are expected to win without running up a total.

The market largely agrees on the headline, pricing Uruguay at 67.5 cents against the model's 65.6%, a near-match. The disagreement sits in the goals. The model sees Over 2.5 at just 32.4% while Polymarket has it up at 46.5 cents, and both-teams-to-score splits even wider: 28.1% on the model versus 41 cents on the market. So the genuine open question here is not who wins but how open it gets — the model expects a cagier, lower-scoring night than the betting crowd does.

Iran come into Los Angeles ranked 21st against an 85th-ranked New Zealand, and the model reads this as a clear-but-not-crushing favourite's game. It hands Iran a 1.46 to 1.01 edge in expected goals, which translates to 46.9% for the win against 25.5% for New Zealand and a hefty 27.6% draw. The form lines pull the same way: Iran arrive off friendly wins over Mali and Gambia, while New Zealand have lost seven of their last ten, including a 0-4 thrashing by Haiti and a narrow defeat to England. Iran are favoured to open the scoring (53.8%), and the single most likely outcome on the model is a 1-1 draw at 13.1%, with 1-0 and a goalless stalemate close behind.

The disagreement with the market is small but consistent. Polymarket prices Iran at 52.5 cents versus the model's 46.9%, trimming the draw and especially New Zealand, whom it rates just 20.5% against the model's 25.5%. The market also leans lower-scoring: it sees over 2.5 goals at 38.5 cents where the model sits at 44.9%, and both-teams-to-score at 43.5 against 49.5%. So the genuine open question is whether New Zealand's defence holds up better than the numbers fear, or folds as their recent results suggest.

France arrive top of the FIFA ranking with a squad valued at €1,520m against Senegal's €478m, and the model reads this as a clear but not crushing edge. It expects 1.42 goals from France and 0.86 from Senegal, which translates to a 49.6% home win, a 28.3% draw and a 22.0% chance Senegal take it. That's a contest, not a coronation: 1-0 (13.9%), 1-1 (13.1%) and 0-0 (10.9%) head the scoreline list, and the model has France leading at half-time only 35.3% of the time against a 46.3% draw. France are favourites to open the scoring at 55.6%, but both teams scoring sits at a live 44.3%, reflecting a Senegal side that beat Egypt and Mali en route through the Africa Cup of Nations.

The market is far less hesitant. Polymarket prices France at 67.5 cents to win, almost eighteen points above the model, and trims Senegal to just 12.5 cents against the model's 22.0%. The two also part ways on goals: the model leans under, pricing over 2.5 at 39.8%, while the market sits near a coin-flip at 49.5 cents. So the genuine open question is whether France are as dominant as the betting suggests, or whether Senegal's quality keeps this tighter and lower-scoring than the favourites' price implies.

This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it that way. Norway carry a squad valued at €589.9m against Iraq's €21.2m, sit 31st to Iraq's 57th in the rankings, and arrive on a strong run that includes a 4-1 win in Italy and a 3-1 over Sweden. The Dixon-Coles numbers reflect the gulf: 2.0 expected goals for Norway against just 0.56 for Iraq, which translates to a 70.99% win for the visitors, 20.06% for the draw and only 8.94% for Iraq. Norway are favoured to open the scoring 71.66% of the time, and the most likely scorelines are all clean Norwegian wins — 0-2 at 15.4%, 0-1 at 15%.

Where it gets interesting is the size of that edge. Polymarket prices Norway at 81.5 cents, a good ten points clear of the model's read, with Iraq down at 6.5 cents and the draw at 13.5. The two also part ways on goals: the model has the over 2.5 as essentially a coin flip at 47.06%, while the market leans over at 61.5 cents. So the direction is agreed — Norway, comfortably — but the market expects a more emphatic, higher-scoring win than the numbers do. That gap is the genuine open question here.

A heavy favourite meeting a side that travels well: that's the shape here. Argentina, ranked third and carrying a squad valued at €782.5m against Algeria's €256.9m, have won eight of their last ten, while Algeria arrive on the back of a 1-0 win in the Netherlands and a run of clean sheets through the Africa Cup of Nations. The model reads it as control rather than a rout, projecting 1.74 expected goals for Argentina and just 0.62 for Algeria, with a 66% chance the hosts strike first. That feeds a 64% home win, a 23.8% draw and only a 12.2% Algeria upset, and the favoured scorelines are tight ones — 1-0 (16%) and 2-0 (14.3%) lead the board.

The market sees the same winner but trusts it more, pricing Argentina at 70.5 cents against the model's 64%, and trimming the draw to 20.5. The sharper split is on goals: Polymarket has over 2.5 at 48.5 cents while the model sits at 41.8%, and the cheaper-end totals lean the same way. So the honest disagreement is about volume, not outcome — the market expects a slightly more open, higher-scoring afternoon than the model, which leans toward a low-scoring Argentina hold. BTTS is close, 38.4% to 41.

Austria arrive as clear favourites, ranked 24th to Jordan's 63rd and carrying a squad valued at €242.2m against €20.3m, and the model reads the matchup the obvious way: a side that has won eight of its last ten driving play against opponents who lost their two most recent friendlies, 0-2 to Colombia and 1-4 to Switzerland. The expected-goals split, 1.95 to 0.78, frames it as control rather than a shootout, and the projected scorelines bear that out — 2-0 (12.4%), 1-0 (12.3%) and 1-1 (10.4%) top the board. Austria are 66.5% to score first and the model gives them a 64.6% win.

That last figure is where the disagreement lives. Polymarket prices Austria at 73.5 cents, nearly nine points clear of the model, and trims the draw to 16.5% and Jordan to 9.5% against the model's 13.6%. The market is simply more convinced this is routine; the model leaves a fatter tail for an upset or a stalemate. The two lines agree closely on goals — over 2.5 sits at 51.4% to the market's 57.5%, and both put both-teams-to-score around 46% — so the open question is margin, not entertainment. Jordan's Arab Cup run, including a 3-0 win at Egypt, argues they are not pushovers.

This is a clear favourite against a stubborn underdog. Portugal sit fifth in the world to DR Congo's 46th, and the squad-value gap is vast — €1,010m against €143.9m — so the model frames it as a question of whether Portugal can break down a side that defends well rather than whether they win. The projected scoreline reads 1.68 to 0.51, and the most likely results are all low and one-sided: 1-0 at 18.4%, 2-0 at 15.8%, with a goalless draw third at 11.7%. Portugal are 67.8% to score first and the model gives them a 65.8% win, leaning on DR Congo's recent miserliness — a 0-0 with Denmark, plus shutout wins over Jamaica and Cameroon.

The disagreement is one of degree, not direction. Polymarket prices Portugal at 76.5 cents to win against the model's 65.8%, treating this as closer to a formality. The same split runs through the goals markets: the model sees over 2.5 at just 37.5% while the market sits at 56.5%, and BTTS at 32.9% against 41 cents. In short, the market expects a more open, higher-scoring afternoon than the numbers do — worth watching if DR Congo's defensive record holds.

England arrive in Dallas as the heavy favourite, and the model leaves little doubt why. A squad valued at €1,360m against Croatia's €387.3m, a number four world ranking against eleven, and a recent run that includes back-to-back clean sheets over Serbia all push expected goals to 1.58 for England and just 0.69 for Croatia. That gap drives a 58.3 percent home win, with the draw at 26.2 percent and Croatia given only 15.5 percent. England are favoured to open the scoring 62 percent of the time, and the most likely scorelines are tellingly cagey — 1-0 at 15.8 percent, 2-0 at 12.9 — pointing to control rather than a goal rush.

This is a low-event projection: over 2.5 goals lands at just 39.6 percent and both teams to score at 40.1 percent, consistent with a side that wins by suffocation. The market broadly agrees on the favourite but trims the edges, pricing England at 56.5 cents and giving Croatia a slightly warmer 18.5 against the model's 15.5. The clearer disagreement is on goals: Polymarket has both teams to score at 47.5 cents and over 2.5 at 44.5, noticeably above the model. Whether Croatia's attack can puncture England's defence is the open question here.

Despite sitting 41 places below Panama in the FIFA rankings, Ghana open this Group L tie as home favourites at BMO Field, and the squad-value gap explains why: their players are worth €234.6m against Panama's €34.55m. The model leans the same way, projecting 1.36 expected goals to 1.03 and giving Ghana a 43.6% win against 27.9% for Panama, with the draw close behind at 28.6%. The recent form lines complicate the picture, though — Ghana's friendly run reads poorly, including a 5-1 thrashing by Austria, while Panama have been competitive, beating South Africa and pushing through CONCACAF qualifying. This shapes up as a tight, modestly-scoring contest: the most likely scorelines are 1-1 (13.5%), 1-0 (11.8%) and a goalless draw (9.8%), and the model splits 0-0 to over-three-goal outcomes almost evenly, with over 2.5 priced at just 42.7%.

There is no real argument with Polymarket here. The market has Ghana at 44.5 cents, the draw at 27.5 and Panama at 27.5, a near-mirror of the model's read, and the totals agree just as tightly — over 2.5 at 41.5 against the model's 42.7%, both-teams-to-score at 48 against 48.4%. When model and market line up this cleanly, the honest call is that the price is fair.

This is a clear favourite-against-outsider setup at the Azteca, and the numbers reflect it. Colombia sit 13th in the world to Uzbekistan's 50th, with a squad valued at €302m against €85m, and the model hands them a 1.5 expected-goals edge over the Uzbeks' 0.63. That translates to a 57.98% win probability for Colombia, 27.11% for the draw and just 14.91% for Uzbekistan. The shape is low-scoring rather than a rout: under 2.5 goals is the likelier outcome at 64%, both teams scoring is only a 36.88% shot, and the model's four most probable scorelines are all tight — 0-1, 0-2, 0-0 and 1-1. Colombia are first to score 61.78% of the time, but a goalless first half (46.89%) is the single most likely halftime state.

Where the model and market part company is on degree. Polymarket prices Colombia at 70.5 cents to win, well clear of the model's 58%, and trims Uzbekistan to 9.5 and the draw to 20.5. The market also expects more goals, pricing over 2.5 at 49 cents against the model's 35.95%. So the disagreement isn't about who wins — it's whether Colombia win comfortably and openly, or grind through a cagey, tight afternoon. Treat that as genuinely open.

The Czech Republic open Group A in Atlanta as clear favourites, and the numbers explain why. Their squad is valued at €188m against South Africa's €49m, they sit 19 places higher in the FIFA ranking, and they arrive on a strong run — five wins in their last six, including draws held against Denmark and Croatia. The model reflects that edge without overstating it: 1.32 expected goals to 0.85, a 47.2% win probability against South Africa's 23.3%, with the draw a live 29.6%. This shapes up as a controlled rather than emphatic favourite's afternoon. The Czechs are likeliest to draw first blood (53.6%), but at half-time the model actually favours parity at 47.7%, hinting at a slow build rather than an early breakaway.

That points to a tight, low-scoring game: under 2.5 goals is the bet, with over 2.5 only 36.9% and 1-0, 1-1 and 0-0 the three most probable scorelines. Here the market pushes back hardest. Polymarket prices the Czechs at 57.5 cents to South Africa's 17.5, a notably bolder favourite than the model's, and leans to more goals — over 2.5 at 44.5 cents against the model's 36.9%. On the win line and the total, then, the market sees a clearer, busier Czech victory than the model will endorse.

Switzerland are the clear favourites at SoFi Stadium, and the gap is built on more than the rankings — 19th against 65th, a squad valued at €332.5m against €151.6m, and a model expecting them to carry the play. The market broadly agrees but shades a touch friendlier to Haiti: Polymarket prices Morocco at 73.5 cents versus the model's 77%, and the draw at 17.5. The sharper split is on goals at both ends — the model puts both teams scoring at 36%, while the market sits at 43.5 cents, a meaningful gap that pivots entirely on whether Haiti can breach a Bounou-led defence that the model expects to keep tight.

Brazil arrive in Miami as heavy favourites and the model sees little reason to argue: a squad valued at €928m against Scotland's €170m, the world's sixth-ranked side against the 43rd, and an expected-goals split of 2.47 to 0.57 that tells the whole story of the matchup. The model gives Brazil a 78.8% chance of winning against just 6.3% for Scotland, with Brazil first to score 77% of the time and leading at the break in well over half of simulations. The most likely outcomes are clean-sheet Brazil wins — 0-2 at 14.6%, 0-3 at 12% and 0-1 at 11.5% lead the scoreline board — and there's a 58.5% chance the game clears 2.5 goals.

The interesting gap is on the moneyline. Polymarket has Brazil at just 66.5 cents, a fair distance below the model's 78.8%, with the draw (19.5 vs 15%) and a Scotland win (14.5 vs 6.3%) both priced more generously than the numbers suggest. That's a genuine disagreement worth flagging rather than resolving: the market is hedging against an upset the model barely entertains. Elsewhere the two are closer — both have both teams scoring around 40-47% and the over 2.5 line hovering near a coin flip.

Mexico arrive at the Azteca as clear favourites against a Czech side that ranks 41st in the world to their 15th, and the model leans hard into the home advantage of a Mexico City crowd: 1.9 expected goals to 0.81. That gap drives a 62.5% Mexico win, with the draw at 22.6% and the Czechs given just 14.9%. Mexico are the more likely team to break the deadlock too, favoured to score first 65% of the time, and the scoreline distribution tells the same story — 0-1, 0-2 and 0-3 all sit among the five most probable results. The Czechs come in unbeaten across their last ten, but those wins came against the likes of Gibraltar, San Marino and Guatemala; Mexico's recent form, including a 5-1 over Serbia, looks sterner.

The market broadly agrees but trims Mexico's edge. Polymarket prices the away win at 54.5 cents against the model's 62.5%, nudging the Czechs up to 19.5 and the draw to 26.5. The bigger split is on goals: the model has Over 2.5 essentially a coin flip at 51%, while the market sits lower at 43 cents, expecting a tighter, lower-scoring game. On both fronts it's a genuine open question rather than a settled one.

South Korea arrive as clear favourites and the model agrees: ranked 25th to South Africa's 60th, with a squad valued at €139m against €49m, they project to 1.58 expected goals to the hosts' 0.68. That gap drives a 58.4% win probability for Korea, against 26.2% for the draw and just 15.4% for South Africa. Korea are tipped to strike first 62% of the time, and the model's likeliest scorelines all lean their way — 0-1 at 15.9%, 0-2 at 13.0%, with a stubborn 0-0 still carrying 11.0%. This reads as a low-volume contest in which the favourite controls territory but doesn't necessarily run riot: over 2.5 goals lands below an even chance at 39.4%, and both teams scoring is only a 39.8% shot, reflecting how rarely the model expects South Africa to find the net.

The market broadly nods along, pricing Korea at 62.5 cents to South Africa's 15.5 — a touch firmer on the favourite than our 58.4%. The clearer disagreement is on goals. Polymarket has both teams to score at 47.5 cents and over 2.5 at 47, versus our 39.8% and 39.4%. The market expects a more open, higher-scoring affair; the model sees South Africa's attack staying quiet.

At MetLife this is a tight, low-scoring affair on paper. The model gives Germany a slim edge — 1.10 expected goals to Ecuador's 0.92 — and the scoreline distribution backs that up, with 1-1, 0-1 and 0-0 bunched together at the top, each landing within a whisker of 14%. That cautious read flows from Ecuador's defensive record: a side that drew 0-0 with Canada and Paraguay and held the Netherlands and Morocco to 1-1 isn't easy to break down, and the model expects a grind. Germany are favoured at 38.9% to Ecuador's 29.2%, but with the draw at 31.8% this is closer to a genuine three-way toss-up than the ranking gap suggests.

That's where the model and the market part ways sharply. Polymarket prices Germany as clear favourites at 55 cents while pushing Ecuador down to 19, a far more lopsided view than the model's near-even split — the most striking disagreement on the card. The two sides also diverge on goals: the model leans under, pricing over 2.5 at just 32.8% and both teams to score at 40.7%, where the market sees a livelier game at 49.5% and 52.5%. Take your pick; the model says tight and open.

This is a mismatch on paper and the numbers don't pretend otherwise. Ivory Coast arrive ranked 34th with a squad valued at €522.1m against Curaçao's €25.78m, and their recent form is the kind that travels: a 2-1 win over France, a 4-0 over South Korea, a 1-0 over Scotland. The model reads it as a comfortable away job, projecting 2.18 expected goals for Ivory Coast against just 0.5 for Curaçao and making them 75.9% to win, with the draw a distant 17.4% and a home win only 6.7%. Ivory Coast are 75.5% to open the scoring, and the single likeliest result is a 0-2 at 16.3%, with 0-1 and 0-3 close behind.

There's very little daylight between the model and Polymarket here, which is itself worth noting — both see the same one-sided contest. The market is fractionally keener on Ivory Coast at 79.5% versus our 75.9%, and a touch higher on goals, pricing over 2.5 at 57 cents against the model's near coin-flip 50.2%. Both agree this is unlikely to be a shootout: BTTS sits at 35.3% on the model and 38.5% in the market. No genuine disagreement, just a shared expectation of a controlled Ivorian win.

Japan come into Dallas as the model's clear favourite, and the form lines explain why. Despite Sweden carrying the more valuable squad on paper — €406m to Japan's €271m — it's Japan riding three straight 1-0 wins over Iceland, England and Scotland, while Sweden have lost five of their last ten and stumbled through European qualifying. The model reads it as a controlled home edge: 1.9 expected goals to 0.84, a 61.95% win probability, and a 64.6% chance of striking first. The likeliest scorelines are tight and Japanese — 1-0 at 11.8%, 2-0 at 11.7% — with both teams scoring rated just under a coin flip at 48.83%.

This is where the market pushes back hard. Polymarket prices Japan at only 47 cents to the model's 62%, and lifts Sweden to 26.5 cents against the model's 15.39% — a wide gap that treats this as far more of an open contest than the numbers suggest. The draw is nudged up too, 27.5 cents versus 22.66%. On totals the two broadly agree, with over 2.5 goals near even (51.68% model, 45.5 cents market). The honest read: the model loves Japan's recent run, the market trusts it noticeably less, and that disagreement is the bet worth watching.

The shape here is a heavy mismatch. The Netherlands arrive ranked 7th against a Tunisia side 44th in the world, and the squad valuations tell the same story: €754m against €69.95m. The model reads it as a clear away job, projecting 1.77 expected goals for the Dutch to Tunisia's 0.64 and pricing the Netherlands at 64% to win, with the draw at 23% and a home win down at just 12%. That's built on form too — the Dutch have lost only twice in their last ten, while Tunisia were beaten 5-0 by Belgium and 1-0 by Austria in their most recent friendlies. The most likely scorelines are all low and Dutch-favoured: 0-1 at 15% and 0-2 at 14%.

On the result, model and market are essentially in lockstep — Polymarket has the Netherlands at 65.5 cents, the draw at 22.5 and Tunisia at 13.5, almost identical to the model. The disagreement is about goals. The market sees this as a coin-flip for over 2.5 (50 cents) where the model has it at 43%, and prices both teams to score at 48.5 cents against the model's 40%. So the open question isn't who wins, but whether Tunisia find the net at all.

Despite the venue tilting toward Turkey's opponent at SoFi Stadium, this is a road game for the United States in name only, and the model treats it as one they should win. It pegs the US as 53 percent favourites against Turkey's 24 percent, with the draw at 23 percent, and builds that from an expected-goals edge of 1.93 to 1.24. The US are first to score 58 percent of the time, and the likeliest single scorelines all favour the visitors: 1-2 at roughly 10 percent, 0-2 near 8 percent, 0-1 close behind. Turkey arrive on a strong run — eight wins in their last ten, including a 2-2 draw away to Spain — but the model sees an American attack that has put up five against Uruguay and three against Senegal as the sharper threat.

The disagreement with the market is stark and worth flagging. Polymarket prices this as a coin toss, 37.5 cents on each side, where the model leans clearly to the US. The two also split on goals: the model has over 2.5 at 61 percent against the market's 50.5. If you trust the numbers, both the result and the total look like value on the same side — but a pick'em market means plenty of sharp money disagrees.

Two evenly-matched, defence-first sides meet at Levi's Stadium, and the model paints this as a tight, low-scoring affair. Total expected goals sit at just 1.78, Australia edging it on xG at 1.01 to Paraguay's 0.77, and that caution shows everywhere in the numbers: over 2.5 goals is only a 26% shot, both teams to score lands at 35%, and the single likeliest outcome is a goalless draw at 18%, with 0-1 close behind at 16%. Australia, ranked 27th to Paraguay's 40th and unbeaten in their last two warm-ups, are made narrow favourites at 40% to win against Paraguay's 26%, with the draw a live 34%.

The sharper story is where the model and the market pull in opposite directions. Polymarket has Paraguay as the home favourite at 45 cents and Australia out at just 26 — almost the mirror image of the model, which prefers the Australians. Someone is wrong here, and with a higher squad value and home billing behind Paraguay against a model leaning on xG and recent form, it's a genuine open question rather than a settled one. The two also split on goals: the market's 49-cent both-teams-to-score and 40-cent over 2.5 expect a livelier game than the model's grind.

Senegal arrive as heavy favourites and the numbers explain why. The model hands them a 1.79 to 0.47 expected-goals edge, the kind of gap you get when a side ranked 14th with a squad valued at €478.1m meets one ranked 57th worth €21.2m. That translates to a 69.3% win probability against just 8.6% for Iraq, with the draw at 22.1%. The shape is a control-and-suffocate job: Senegal are 70.6% to score first, and the likeliest scorelines are all clean sheets — 1-0 at 18.2%, 2-0 at 16.7%, then 3-0 at 10.0%. Iraq's resilience is real, though; they held Spain 1-1 and Saudi Arabia 0-0 recently, and a 0-0 here carries 10.9%.

On the headline price there is essentially no argument: Polymarket has Senegal at 69.5c, almost exactly the model's read. The disagreement lives in the goals markets. The model sees this as a tighter, lower-scoring game than the market does — over 2.5 goals at 39.3% against 48.5c, and both teams to score at just 31.7% versus a punchy 45c. If you trust Iraq's defensive record, the model's caution looks well placed; if Senegal's firepower runs free, the market's appetite for goals will be vindicated. That is the genuine open question.

France arrive as the world's top-ranked side and the model treats this as their game to lose, pricing them at 54.2% to win against 20.6% for Norway and a 25.2% draw. The expected-goals split tells the same story, 1.69 to 0.96, and France are favoured to open the scoring at 59.1%. None of that makes Norway harmless: a squad worth €589.9m has won six of its last ten and put four past Italy in qualifying, and the model still gives them a one-in-five shout. But the shape here is a stronger, deeper France — squad value €1.52bn, ranked first to Norway's 31st — probing a side that may spend long spells defending. A 1-1 is the single likeliest score at 12%, with 0-1 and 0-2 close behind.

The market and the model are reading this almost identically, which is its own kind of signal. Polymarket has France a fraction shorter on the moneyline at 52.5 cents and Norway slightly longer at 22.5, the only real nudge being marginally more faith in the draw. Totals are a near-perfect match — over 2.5 sits at 49.4% on the model and 49 cents on the market — and both lean to roughly coin-flip both-teams-to-score, 50.8% against 53.5 cents. No edge worth chasing; just agreement.

This is a heavyweight against a grinder. Spain arrive ranked second in the world, riding a long unbeaten run built on 4-0 and 6-0 qualifying scorelines, with a squad valued at €1.22bn against Uruguay's €359m. The model reflects that gap without treating it as a foregone conclusion: it gives Spain 1.37 expected goals to Uruguay's 0.7 and makes them 52.3% to win, with the draw a live 29.1% and Uruguay just 18.6%. The shape it expects is a controlled Spanish edge rather than a rout — Uruguay's recent form is a wall of clean sheets and goalless draws (Algeria, Mexico, Chile), and the model leans that way too, with 0-1, 0-0 and 1-1 its three likeliest scores and a 13.2% chance nobody scores at all.

The interesting friction is in the goals markets. The model sees a tight, low-scoring game: over 2.5 at just 34.4% and both teams to score at 38.3%. Polymarket disagrees sharply, pricing over 2.5 at 49 cents and BTTS at 51.5 — essentially coin flips. The market also nudges Spain's win price up to 59.5 against the model's 52.3. So treat the winner as a genuine open question, but note the clear split on how open the game itself will be.

This is about as level as a World Cup group game gets. The model splits it almost perfectly three ways — Cape Verde 32.5%, the draw 33.8%, Saudi Arabia 33.7% — with expected goals of just 0.91 to 0.93 a side, the signature of two evenly matched teams who don't promise many chances. That low-scoring read runs right through the numbers: over 2.5 goals lands at only 27.9%, both teams to score at 36.8%, and the single most likely outcome on the board is a 0-0 at 16.6%, narrowly ahead of 0-1 and 1-1. Cape Verde arrive on the back of 3-0 friendly wins over Bermuda and Serbia; Saudi Arabia's form is choppier, but their 3-0 in Puerto Rico keeps them live.

The clearest gap with the market is on goals. Polymarket prices over 2.5 at 44 cents and both teams to score at 52.5, comfortably above the model's 27.9% and 36.8% — the market expects a more open game than the numbers do. On the result, Polymarket leans faintly toward Cape Verde at 37.5 cents and trims the draw to 28.5, where the model actually rates the stalemate as the most likely single outcome. Treat both as genuinely open.

This is the closest thing to a coin flip Group G offers. The model gives Egypt 34.5%, the draw 32.6% and Iran 32.95%, with projected goals almost identical at 0.99 to 0.96 — a tight, low-event game between sides that defend better than they create. That low ceiling runs through the totals: over 2.5 lands at just 31.2%, both teams to score at 39.7%, and the single most likely scoreline is 0-0 at 14.8%, narrowly ahead of 1-1. Egypt's slim edge rests partly on a squad valued at €116m against Iran's €32m, though Iran sit eight places higher in the FIFA ranking at 21st, which keeps the matchup honest.

The disagreement is on Egypt's price. Polymarket has them at 43.5 cents to win, against the model's 34.5%, and shades Iran out to 26.5 where the model holds 33%. So the market sees Egypt as clear favourites and the model sees three roughly equal outcomes — a genuine open question rather than evidence either way. The market also leans slightly more towards goals, pricing over 2.5 at 35.5 and both teams scoring at 44.5, both above the model. If you trust the numbers, the value is on Iran's chances and on the game staying tight.

A mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. Belgium come in ranked 9th to New Zealand's 85th, with a squad valued at €547.5m against €34.35m, and recent form to match: five wins in their last six including 5-0 over Tunisia and 2-0 at Croatia, while the All Whites have lost eight of their last ten, shipping four to Haiti only weeks ago. The model projects 2.27 expected goals for Belgium and just 0.72 for New Zealand, and turns that into a 72.1% Belgium win, 18.1% draw and only 9.8% for the hosts. The most likely scorelines all flatter the favourite — 0-2 at 13.0%, 0-1 at 11.1%, 0-3 at 9.8% — and Belgium are favoured to open the scoring 71.8% of the time.

There's little daylight between model and market here. Polymarket prices Belgium at 76.5 cents, a touch firmer than the model's 72.1%, while clipping New Zealand to 8.5 cents and the draw to 16. On the goals, agreement is near-total: over 2.5 sits at 57.3% on the model and 56.5 cents on the market, with both-teams-to-score split 46.3% to 44. No real edge to flag — just a heavy favourite both sides price the same way.

Croatia walk into Lincoln Financial Field as clear favourites, and the gap looks structural: a FIFA ranking of 11 against Ghana's 74, a squad valued at €387.3m against €234.6m, and a model expecting 1.6 goals to Ghana's 0.75. The shape is a familiar one — a settled European side controlling possession against opponents who arrive on the back of a rough run of friendlies, having shipped five to Austria and two each to Mexico and Japan in recent months. The model puts Croatia at 57.1% to win, the draw at 26.1% and Ghana at just 16.8%, and it likes them to open the scoring (61.2% first to score). The single most probable outcome is a tidy 1-0, with 2-0 and 1-1 close behind.

This isn't a goalfest in the numbers. Over 2.5 lands at only 41.6% and both teams to score at 42.6%, consistent with Croatia keeping things quiet. The market broadly agrees but leans a touch more emphatic on the favourite, pricing Croatia at 60 cents versus the model's 57. The clearer disagreement is on goals: Polymarket has both-teams-to-score at 48 cents against the model's 42.6%, treating a Ghana reply as more live than we do.

This is a mismatch the model treats as a near-formality. England arrive ranked fourth in the world against a 33rd-ranked Panama, and the squad values tell the same story in starker terms: €1.36bn against €34.55m. The Dixon-Coles engine projects 2.86 expected goals for England and just 0.44 for Panama, which feeds an 86.2% England win, a 10.5% draw and only a 3.3% home win. England are favoured to open the scoring 83.2% of the time and to lead at the break 65.1%. The most likely scorelines are all clean-sheet England wins — 0-2 at 15%, 0-3 at 14.3% — and over 2.5 goals lands at 64.2%, befitting a one-sided contest where the question is margin rather than result.

The market broadly agrees but tempers the certainty. Polymarket has England at 75.5 cents to the model's 86.2%, with the draw fatter at 15.5 cents against 10.5%, and Panama nudged up to 10.5 cents from 3.3% — a meaningful gap on the underdog and the draw. The clearest split is on both teams to score: the model sees 34.1%, the market 44.5%, suggesting backers give Panama more credit to find a goal than the numbers do. Worth watching, but the headline verdict is shared.

Portugal arrive in Miami as the heavier name — fifth in the FIFA ranking to Colombia's 13th, and a squad valued at just over a billion euros against Colombia's €302m — and the model reads the matchup that way without overcommitting. It gives Portugal a 1.36 expected-goals edge to Colombia's 1.05 and makes them favourites at 43.2%, with the draw and a Colombia win almost level behind at 28.5% and 28.4%. This is a close, low-scoring profile: over 2.5 goals lands at just 43.3%, the most likely scoreline is a 1-1 at 13.5%, and Portugal are marginally the side more likely to open it, scoring first 51% of the time. Both teams arrive in form, Colombia unbeaten in their last four friendlies and Portugal beaten only once in their last ten.

The market broadly agrees but leans a touch harder on Portugal, pricing the away win at 46.5 cents against the model's 43.2% — a real if modest gap that frames this as a live question rather than a settled one. The clearer disagreement is on goals at both ends: Polymarket has both teams to score at 53 cents, comfortably above the model's 49%, so the market expects a slightly more open game than the numbers do.

Two evenly matched debutant-tier sides meet in Atlanta, and the model sees a tight, low-event game. The expected goals tell the story: 0.73 for DR Congo, 0.78 for Uzbekistan, virtually nothing between them and not much for either. That pushes the draw to the front of the moneyline at 37.9 percent, ahead of Uzbekistan at 32.5 and DR Congo at 29.5, despite the Congolese carrying the higher FIFA ranking (46 to 50) and the heavier squad, valued at €143.9m against €85.3m. This shapes up as a goalkeeping afternoon: a 0-0 is the single most likely scoreline at 22.8 percent, both teams to score sits at just 28.6 percent, and the model gives only a 19.2 percent chance of more than two and a half goals.

That last number is where the market pushes back hardest. Polymarket prices over 2.5 goals at 40.5 cents, more than double the model's read, and both-teams-to-score at 47.5 against the model's 28.6. The market also leans firmly toward DR Congo at 41 cents, where the model has a coin-flippier three-way. So the genuine disagreement is twofold: the market expects more goals and more from the Congolese. Treat both as open questions rather than settled facts.

Algeria get the nominal home billing in Kansas City and the model leans their way, projecting 1.33 expected goals to Austria's 1.02 and pricing the hosts at 43% to win against 28% for Austria, with 29% draws. The case is built on form: Algeria beat the Netherlands 1-0 in June and ran deep into the Africa Cup of Nations, while their ranking (28th) sits just behind Austria's (24th) and squad values are near-level at €256.9m to €242.2m. This is a tight, low-volume game in the model's eyes — over 2.5 goals lands at just 42%, both teams to score at 48%, and the single likeliest outcome is a 1-1 draw at 14%, with 1-0 and 0-0 close behind.

The disagreement here is stark and worth flagging. Polymarket has it the other way round, making Austria the favourite at 44.5 cents and Algeria the outsider at just 25.5 — almost a mirror image of the model's read. Austria's qualifying run, including a 5-1 friendly win over Ghana and a settled defence around Alaba and Danso, is clearly weighing on the market. On the goals markets the two broadly agree — over 2.5 within a point either side — so the real question is purely who wins, and on that the model and the money genuinely diverge.

This is as lopsided as group J gets. Argentina, ranked third in the world against Jordan's 63rd, carry a squad valued at €782.5m to Jordan's €20.3m, and the model reads the gap accordingly: an expected goals line of 3.32 to 0.36 and a 91.5% Argentina win. Jordan's path is barely a crack of light at 1.67%, the draw 6.83%. The shape is one-directional. Argentina are 87.9% to score first and lead 71.9% of the time at the break, and Jordan's recent form fits the picture — beaten 4-1 by Switzerland and 2-0 by Colombia in their last two friendlies after a strong Arab Cup run. The likeliest scorelines are 0-3, 0-2 and 0-4.

Where there's room for argument is the margin, not the winner. The market is more cautious on Argentina, pricing them at 82.5 cents against the model's 91.5%, and it leans toward a tighter, more open game: it has both teams to score at 39 cents versus the model's 29.18%, and over 2.5 goals at 60.5 against 71.16%. So the disagreement is real but narrow — the model expects Argentina to bury Jordan, the market expects them to win while conceding the odd chance.
